title: Banana Bread

ingredients.name: very ripe bananas (peeled, mashed)
visual_checks: skins should be heavily speckled or black — the riper, the sweeter
ingredients.notes: skins should be heavily speckled or black — the riper, the sweeter

subs.name: 1.5 cups whole wheat flour (atta) for a nuttier, fibre-rich loaf; add 2 extra tbsp milk to compensate for the drier flour
1/3 cup coconut oil or neutral vegetable oil — equally moist
2 tbsp thick curd per egg for an eggless version — the loaf will be slightly denser but still delicious
50 g dark chocolate chips or 3 tbsp desiccated coconut for an egg-free version without nut allergies

steps.text: Preheat oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease a standard 23×13 cm loaf tin with butter or oil and line the base with a strip of parchment paper.
Peel the bananas and mash them thoroughly in a large bowl with a fork until almost smooth — a few small lumps are welcome as they create pockets of soft banana in the finished loaf.
Stir the melted butter into the mashed banana. Add sugar, beaten eggs, and vanilla extract and mix until the batter is uniform and slightly glossy.
Sprinkle the baking soda and salt over the batter and stir to distribute evenly.

Add the maida all at once. Fold gently with a spatula until just incorporated — the batter will look shaggy and lumpy; this is correct. Stop folding the moment no dry flour streaks remain, as overmixing develops gluten and results in a rubbery loaf.
Fold in the walnuts if using, then pour the batter into the prepared tin and smooth the top lightly.

Bake for 55–60 minutes until the top is deeply golden-brown and cracked down the centre, and a toothpick inserted in the middle comes out with only moist crumbs — not wet batter. Check at 50 minutes; ovens vary.
Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then lift out by the parchment lining and transfer to a wire rack. Slice only once the loaf is fully cool — a warm loaf will compress and tear.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → 1/3 cup coconut oil or neutral vegetable oil — equally moist; egg → 2 tbsp thick curd per egg for an eggless version — the loaf will be slightly denser but still delicious; walnut → 50 g dark chocolate chips or 3 tbsp desiccated coconut for an egg-free version without nut allergies; maida → 1.5 cups whole wheat flour (atta) for a nuttier, fibre-rich loaf; add 2 extra tbsp milk to compensate for the drier flour
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Bake in an OTG at 175°C, or use the pressure-cooker tin method: preheat with salt/sand base (no gasket, no whistle) for 10 minutes on medium, then bake on low flame for 65–70 minutes. Use Amul butter or refined oil. Very ripe 'rasthali' or 'robusta' bananas sold cheaply at the market are ideal — the darker and softer the skin, the sweeter the bread.
